reasoning: Mahmoud Khalil, a lawful permanent resident, is facing a deportation effort by the Trump administration under a rare foreign policy provision. While a Louisiana immigration judge ruled on April 11, 2025, that he is deportable, his deportation is highly unlikely to be executed before July 1, 2025. Khalil is represented by experienced counsel who have already initiated multiple legal avenues to delay and challenge his removal. First, he has pending opportunities for immigration relief, such as an asylum claim, which would necessitate further immigration court proceedings. Second, his lawyers have committed to appealing any adverse final decision to the Board of Immigration Appeals (BIA), which typically takes many months to resolve; during this time, he cannot be deported. Third, he has active federal habeas corpus litigation ongoing in New Jersey (as of May 7, a federal judge ordered the administration to brief its legal precedents), and federal judges in NY and NJ have previously issued stays of removal prohibiting his deportation while litigation is pending. The combination of mandatory administrative appeals (BIA), ongoing federal court litigation, existing judicial orders pausing his removal, and the very short timeline (~6 weeks from mid-May to early July) make it exceedingly unlikely he will be deported before July 1, 2025.
